era_context: The Middle Bronze Age (MBA) in the Levant and Egypt; the Old Babylonian period in Mesopotamia. Egypt's 13th Dynasty was in decline; Hyksos infiltration into the Delta was ongoing. In Mesopotamia, Hammurabi had died c. 1750 BCE; his son Samsu-iluna (r. c. 1749–1712 BCE) was ruling Babylon and facing military challenges from neighboring states. The Indus Valley Civilization was in its mature phase. No major civilizational shifts are documented for this specific year.

events.title: Samsu-iluna of Babylon — reign continuation and military campaigns
events.description: Samsu-iluna, son and successor of Hammurabi, continued to rule Babylon during this period. His reign (c. 1749–1712 BCE) was marked by military campaigns against neighboring states, including the Kassites to the east and rebellious cities within Babylonian territory. Samsu-iluna faced significant military pressure and was less successful than his father in maintaining Babylonian hegemony. By the end of his reign, Babylon's territorial control had contracted significantly. The specific events of -1728 are not individually documented, but this year falls within his reign of military consolidation and decline.
key_figures: Samsu-iluna (King of Babylon, r. c. 1749–1712 BCE)

sources.name: Cuneiform inscriptions and administrative records from Babylon
Historiographic consensus on Old Babylonian chronology

events.certainty_note: Samsu-iluna's reign dates and general military activities are well-attested in cuneiform sources. However, specific events in -1728 are not individually documented. The assignment of this year to his reign is secure, but no discrete event is recorded for this particular year.
cross_references: Part of the Old Babylonian period (c. 1894–1595 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: Samsu-iluna's reign is attested in cuneiform sources, but detailed year-by-year records are sparse. The Babylonian King List provides reign lengths and some military campaigns, but specific events in -1728 are not documented. The broader context of post-Hammurabi decline is well-established, but this year falls in a period of limited documentation for discrete events.

graph_edges.note: Samsu-iluna's reign began after Hammurabi's death c. 1750 BCE
